questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "In the name of the most serene, and most powerful prince, King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella... we notify and make known to you as best we can, that God our Lord, Living and Eternal, created the Heaven and the Earth... and one man and one woman... of whom you and we, and all the men of the world were and are descendants... [The Pope] made donation of these isles and Tierra-firme to the aforesaid King and Queen and to their successors." — The Requerimiento, c. 1513

questions.question: The Requerimiento was most directly used to justify which Spanish colonial practice?

choices.text: The encomienda system granting colonists authority over indigenous labor
choices.explanation: The Requerimiento was read aloud to indigenous peoples to justify Spanish conquest and the extraction of labor through the encomienda, claiming papal authority for domination.

choices.text: The conversion of indigenous peoples to Islam as a condition for trade rights
choices.explanation: The Requerimiento invoked Christian authority and the Pope — not Islam — as the basis for Spanish colonial claims.

choices.text: The establishment of democratic assemblies in New Spain
choices.explanation: The Requerimiento established hierarchical colonial authority, not democratic governance structures.

choices.text: The abolition of indigenous tribute systems in favor of free markets
choices.explanation: The Requerimiento actually legitimized tribute extraction from indigenous peoples, not its abolition.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how the exchange of ideas, commodities, diseases, and people transformed European and non-European societies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The Indians [of Hispaniola] could no longer endure it and they began to flee to the mountains... The Spaniards... cut off the legs of some of them, and the hands and noses of others... These cruelties and others like them are still practiced at the present day, and will be practiced as long as there are Indians here." — Bartolomé de las Casas, A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies, 1542

questions.question: Las Casas's account is best understood as evidence of which broader historical process?

choices.text: The demographic collapse of indigenous populations caused by Spanish colonial violence and exploitation
choices.explanation: Las Casas documented the systematic violence and coerced labor that, combined with disease, reduced indigenous Caribbean populations by an estimated 90% within a century of contact.

choices.text: The voluntary assimilation of indigenous peoples into Spanish colonial society
choices.explanation: The passage describes coercion and atrocities — the opposite of voluntary assimilation — and Las Casas was explicitly condemning Spanish violence, not documenting peaceful integration.

choices.text: The successful resistance of indigenous populations against Spanish colonizers
choices.explanation: Las Casas describes flight and defeat, not successful resistance. The passage emphasizes the vulnerability of indigenous people to Spanish violence.

choices.text: The failure of the Catholic Church to establish missions in the New World
choices.explanation: Las Casas was himself a priest advocating for indigenous rights — the Church did establish missions. His work demonstrates internal Catholic debate about colonial ethics, not mission failure.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Columbian Exchange.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
| Commodity | Direction of Transfer | Impact |
|-----------|----------------------|--------|
| Smallpox, measles, influenza | Europe → Americas | ~90% indigenous population decline |
| Maize, potatoes, tomatoes | Americas → Europe/Asia/Africa | Caloric expansion, population growth |
| Horses, cattle, pigs | Europe → Americas | Transformed indigenous economies |
| Sugar, coffee | Americas (sugar) / Asia (coffee) → Global | Drove Atlantic slave trade |

questions.question: Which conclusion is best supported by the table above?

choices.text: The Columbian Exchange had symmetric benefits for all regions involved
choices.explanation: The table shows highly asymmetric outcomes — disease transfer caused catastrophic indigenous population loss, while European populations grew from American crops. Benefits were profoundly unequal.

choices.text: The Columbian Exchange transformed demographic, ecological, and economic systems across multiple continents
choices.explanation: The table shows disease killing ~90% of indigenous peoples, crops transforming global caloric supply, animals reshaping American economies, and cash crops fueling the slave trade — all reflecting massive multi-continental transformation.

choices.text: European diseases had no significant long-term impact on indigenous societies
choices.explanation: The table explicitly states ~90% indigenous population decline — one of the greatest demographic catastrophes in human history, with profound long-term consequences.

choices.text: American crops were adopted only in Europe and had no impact on Asia or Africa
choices.explanation: The table specifies that crops like maize and potatoes spread to Europe, Asia, and Africa, becoming staple foods that drove population growth across all three continents.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Columbian Exchange.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "He [Columbus] had promised his men that if they had not sighted land within a few days he would turn back. On the morning of October 12, 1492, they sighted an island. Columbus claimed it for Spain... calling it San Salvador." — Adapted from a 16th-century Spanish chronicle

questions.question: Columbus's 1492 voyage most directly resulted in which long-term historical consequence?

choices.text: The permanent integration of the Americas into global trade and demographic networks, fundamentally reshaping all interconnected societies
choices.explanation: Columbus's contact initiated the Columbian Exchange and Atlantic World trade system, permanently connecting previously isolated hemispheres and transforming economics, demographics, and cultures globally.

choices.text: The immediate establishment of a lasting peace between European powers over American territories
choices.explanation: Columbus's voyage instead triggered intense European rivalry over American territories, including the Treaty of Tordesillas and centuries of colonial competition between Spain, Portugal, France, England, and the Netherlands.

choices.text: The discovery of a direct sea route from Europe to Asia as Columbus intended
choices.explanation: Columbus never reached Asia — he reached the Americas, which he initially mistook for Asia. The western route to Asia remained Columbus's unfulfilled goal.

choices.text: The decline of indigenous trade networks already in place across the Americas
choices.explanation: While indigenous trade did decline after European contact, this was a secondary effect of the broader demographic and colonial consequences, not the most direct or significant long-term result.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of European exploration and colonization.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The Middle Passage refers to which aspect of the Atlantic slave trade?

choices.text: The sea voyage transporting enslaved Africans from Africa to the Americas
choices.explanation: The Middle Passage was the second leg of the triangular trade — the brutal transatlantic voyage from West Africa to the Americas during which millions of enslaved people died.

choices.text: The overland route connecting West African slave markets to the Mediterranean
choices.explanation: The Middle Passage was specifically the transatlantic sea route, not an overland route. The trans-Saharan slave trade predated it and operated separately.

choices.text: The processing of enslaved people in Caribbean ports before sale to mainland colonists
choices.explanation: Port processing was called 'seasoning' by slavers, not the Middle Passage. The Middle Passage refers specifically to the ocean crossing from Africa.

choices.text: The trade route from Europe to Africa carrying manufactured goods in exchange for enslaved people
choices.explanation: That was the first leg of the triangular trade. The Middle Passage was specifically the crossing from Africa to the Americas — the 'middle' leg of the triangle.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Atlantic slave trade.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The Portuguese... trade in this country [West Africa] is very great... They bring from Lisbon cloth of many kinds, linen cloth, silver, and many other goods... and take away slaves, gold, pepper, ivory... The trade is so great that it is scarcely to be believed." — Duarte Pacheco Pereira, Esmeraldo de Situ Orbis, c. 1508

questions.question: The trading pattern described in the passage most directly contributed to which long-term development?

choices.text: The development of a triangular trade system connecting Europe, Africa, and the Americas
choices.explanation: Portuguese trade with West Africa for slaves and gold pioneered the commercial relationships that would expand into the full triangular Atlantic trade system once the Americas were incorporated.

choices.text: The decline of Muslim merchants' role in trans-Saharan gold trade
choices.explanation: While Portuguese sea trade did gradually undercut trans-Saharan commerce, this passage specifically describes the bilateral trade pattern that fed into the Atlantic system — not the displacement of Muslim merchants as the primary outcome.

choices.text: The voluntary migration of West Africans to Portuguese colonies in South America
choices.explanation: The passage describes the purchase of slaves — forced displacement, not voluntary migration. The slave trade was characterized by coercion and violence.

choices.text: The establishment of Portuguese sovereignty over all West African kingdoms
choices.explanation: Portugal established coastal trading posts, not territorial sovereignty over West African kingdoms. African rulers often controlled the terms of inland trade.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Atlantic slave trade.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: How did the encomienda system differ from outright slavery as practiced in the Atlantic system?

choices.text: Encomienda granted Spanish colonists labor rights over indigenous peoples who remained legally free, while Atlantic slavery treated enslaved Africans as property
choices.explanation: Encomienda technically preserved indigenous people's legal status as subjects of the Crown, granting colonists tribute and labor rights — but in practice it often differed little from slavery in its exploitation. Atlantic slavery treated enslaved people as chattel property.

choices.text: Encomienda was based on voluntary labor contracts while Atlantic slavery involved no contracts
choices.explanation: Encomienda was coercive, not voluntary. Indigenous people had no meaningful choice. Neither system was based on genuinely voluntary agreements.

choices.text: Encomienda applied only to African workers while Atlantic slavery targeted indigenous peoples
choices.explanation: This is reversed. Encomienda applied to indigenous peoples; the Atlantic slave trade primarily trafficked enslaved Africans. After demographic collapse, Africans replaced indigenous labor.

choices.text: Encomienda was limited to mining while Atlantic slavery operated exclusively on sugar plantations
choices.explanation: Encomienda was used in both mining and agriculture. Atlantic slavery operated across many industries — sugar, tobacco, rice, cotton — not exclusively sugar.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how colonial labor systems developed and changed.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "Bartolomé de las Casas... proposed that the Spaniards be permitted to bring in Black slaves, who would be less exposed to the rigors of the climate, more robust, and more profitable for labor." — Antonio de Herrera y Tordesillas, 1601, describing Las Casas's 1516 proposal

questions.question: This passage is most useful to historians studying which historical development?

choices.text: The interconnected nature of indigenous rights advocacy and the expansion of African slavery
choices.explanation: This passage illustrates how even advocates for indigenous rights like Las Casas could inadvertently or directly support the substitution of African enslaved labor, revealing the complex and interconnected origins of the Atlantic slave trade.

choices.text: The role of African leaders in initiating the Atlantic slave trade
choices.explanation: The passage focuses on a Spanish priest's proposal to a European colonial authority — not on African leaders' agency in the slave trade's origins, though African rulers did participate in slave capture and sale.

choices.text: The success of Las Casas in ending indigenous exploitation in the Spanish colonies
choices.explanation: Las Casas's advocacy had limited success — the New Laws of 1542 were partially revoked due to colonist pressure, and indigenous exploitation continued throughout the colonial period.

choices.text: The environmental reasons why Spanish colonists preferred African laborers for sugar cultivation
choices.explanation: While the passage mentions climate arguments, historians primarily use this passage to understand the moral contradictions and interconnections of colonial labor policies, not simply environmental labor preferences.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Atlantic slave trade.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why silver mined at Potosí (Peru) contributed to inflation in Ming Dynasty China?

choices.text: Ming China required silver to pay taxes, creating massive demand that pulled global silver supplies eastward, increasing China's money supply and driving prices up
choices.explanation: Ming China's 'Single Whip Law' required tax payments in silver, creating enormous demand. American silver flowed through Manila Galleons across the Pacific, increasing China's money supply and causing the inflation known as the 'Price Revolution' in China.

choices.text: Spanish merchants sold Potosí silver directly to Chinese artisans, who used it to produce luxury goods that competed with European markets
choices.explanation: Silver flowed to China primarily through trade, not artisan investment. Chinese artisans consumed some silver, but the inflationary pressure came from silver's role as currency.

choices.text: The Ming government melted American silver to produce copper coins, devaluing China's existing currency
choices.explanation: Silver was the currency, not a raw material for copper production. The Ming government actually transitioned away from paper money to silver — the opposite of copper coin production.

choices.text: Potosí silver funded Chinese military expansion that disrupted agricultural production, causing food prices to rise
choices.explanation: Silver did not fund Chinese military expansion. The inflationary effect was monetary — more silver in circulation relative to goods — not caused by military disruption of agriculture.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how the silver trade connected global economies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The Manila Galleon trade route connected which two regions?

choices.text: Spanish America (Acapulco) and the Philippines/China
choices.explanation: The Manila Galleon sailed annually from Acapulco, Mexico to Manila, Philippines, carrying American silver to exchange for Chinese silk and porcelain, linking the Pacific in a major global trade network.

choices.text: Portugal and West Africa
choices.explanation: This describes the Portuguese trade route along the African coast — not the Manila Galleon, which was a Spanish Pacific trade route.

choices.text: England and the Caribbean sugar islands
choices.explanation: This describes British Caribbean trade. The Manila Galleon was a Spanish Pacific trade route from Mexico to the Philippines.

choices.text: The Ottoman Empire and India
choices.explanation: Ottoman-Indian trade occurred overland through the Middle East or via the Red Sea and Persian Gulf — not through the Manila Galleon, which was a Spanish Pacific route.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how the silver trade connected global economies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The Dutch... made it a point of honor never to pay a higher price than the seller's first asking price, while the Portuguese paid at the second price and the English at the third. But whatever the price, all arrived at the same conclusion — the native was cheated." — A Dutch colonial administrator in the East Indies, c. 1700

questions.question: The Dutch colonial administrator's observation best illustrates which broader pattern of European commercial expansion?

choices.text: European traders consistently exploited unequal power relationships to extract resources from Asian producers at below-market prices
choices.explanation: The passage reveals that regardless of negotiating style, all European traders used their commercial and military power to underpay local producers — illustrating the exploitative nature of European commercial imperialism in Asia.

choices.text: European commercial competition in Asia benefited local producers by driving up prices through bidding wars
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states that 'whatever the price, the native was cheated' — competition among European traders did not benefit local producers.

choices.text: The Dutch VOC was more ethical in its commercial dealings than Portuguese or English traders
choices.explanation: The passage's irony is that even the 'honorable' Dutch system still resulted in cheating local producers — all European trading companies exploited their power advantage.

choices.text: Asian merchants were unable to participate in trade networks alongside European companies
choices.explanation: Asian merchants remained active in trade networks throughout the early modern period. The passage describes exploitation of sellers, not their exclusion from trade.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the role of joint-stock companies in European colonization.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: How did the Dutch VOC (Vereenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie) represent a new form of commercial organization in the early modern period?

choices.text: It was the first joint-stock company to raise capital from multiple investors, share profits, and hold a state-granted monopoly on trade
choices.explanation: The VOC (founded 1602) pioneered the joint-stock model: pooling investment from many shareholders, distributing dividends, and operating under a Dutch state monopoly on Asian trade — innovations that became the foundation of modern capitalism.

choices.text: It was the first company to abolish slavery in its colonial territories as a condition of shareholder investment
choices.explanation: The VOC used enslaved and coerced labor extensively in its colonies. It did not abolish slavery — that development came centuries later.

choices.text: It operated exclusively through diplomatic treaties rather than military force
choices.explanation: The VOC used extensive military force to establish monopolies — famously massacring the population of the Banda Islands for nutmeg control. Military coercion was central to its commercial model.

choices.text: It was the first company to establish trade relationships with China and Japan simultaneously
choices.explanation: Portuguese and earlier traders had established contacts with East Asia before the VOC. The VOC's innovation was organizational — the joint-stock structure — not the geographic scope of its trade.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the role of joint-stock companies in European colonization.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The casta system in New Spain established social hierarchies based on ancestry: peninsulares (born in Spain) ranked highest, followed by criollos (European descent, born in Americas), then mestizos (mixed European-indigenous), mulatos (mixed European-African), and indigenous and African peoples at the bottom." — Adapted from colonial records

questions.question: The casta system described in the passage most directly reflected which broader colonial dynamic?

choices.text: Spanish attempts to maintain hierarchical control by linking legal privilege to racial ancestry and place of birth
choices.explanation: The casta system formalized racial hierarchy as a tool of colonial administration — those with 'pure' European blood born in Spain held the highest privileges, maintaining Spanish dominance while regulating access to offices, occupations, and legal standing.

choices.text: The successful assimilation of African and indigenous peoples into full Spanish colonial citizenship
choices.explanation: The casta system explicitly excluded indigenous and African peoples from full citizenship and legal privilege — it was a system of stratification, not assimilation.

choices.text: A democratic social structure that allowed upward mobility based on individual merit and religious conversion
choices.explanation: The casta system was explicitly based on ancestry, not merit. While religious conversion was important, it did not override racial categorization in colonial hierarchies.

choices.text: The Spanish Crown's effort to eliminate indigenous identity and culture through forced intermarriage
choices.explanation: The casta system actually categorized mixed-race people separately — it was not designed to eliminate indigenous identity through forced intermarriage, but to stratify the mixed-race population that emerged from colonial contact.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how social hierarchies developed in European colonial societies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Vasco da Gama's 1498 voyage to India most directly challenged the dominance of which existing trade network?

choices.text: The Ottoman-controlled eastern Mediterranean trade routes connecting Asian spices to European markets
choices.explanation: Da Gama's sea route around Africa to India bypassed the Ottoman-controlled land and sea routes through the Middle East, threatening the Ottoman monopoly on spice transit and the profits of Muslim merchants and Venetian middlemen.

choices.text: The trans-Saharan trade routes carrying gold and salt between West Africa and North Africa
choices.explanation: Da Gama's Indian Ocean route did not directly challenge trans-Saharan trade, which carried different commodities and served different markets. Portuguese West African coastal trade was more relevant to that network.

choices.text: The Silk Road overland routes connecting China to Central Asia
choices.explanation: While the Portuguese sea route did reduce Silk Road traffic over time, da Gama's immediate competitive challenge was to the spice trade middlemen in the Middle East and Mediterranean, not Central Asian overland traders.

choices.text: The intra-Asian trade networks controlled by Chinese merchants
choices.explanation: Chinese merchants operated primarily within intra-Asian trade; da Gama's route most immediately threatened Ottoman and Muslim merchants who profited from transiting spices from India to Europe.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of European exploration.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following foods, originating in the Americas, most significantly expanded food supplies in Africa and Asia after the Columbian Exchange?

choices.text: Maize and potatoes
choices.explanation: Maize and potatoes, both indigenous to the Americas, became staple crops across Africa, Europe, and Asia after the Columbian Exchange. Their high caloric yields contributed to global population growth from the 16th century onward.

choices.text: Rice and wheat
choices.explanation: Rice originated in Asia and wheat in the Middle East — both predate the Columbian Exchange and are Old World crops, not New World crops transferred to the Eastern Hemisphere.

choices.text: Sugar and coffee
choices.explanation: Sugar originated in South/Southeast Asia (though planted in the Americas). Coffee originated in Ethiopia. Neither is an American crop transferred to the Old World.

choices.text: Wheat and barley
choices.explanation: Wheat and barley are Old World grains that originated in the Middle East thousands of years before European contact with the Americas.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Columbian Exchange.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The conquest of Mexico was accomplished in two years by Hernán Cortés with a force of only about 500 soldiers. The explanation lies not merely in European military technology but in the devastating epidemic diseases that preceded the Spanish advance and the military alliances Cortés formed with indigenous peoples who resented Aztec domination." — Adapted from a modern historical analysis

questions.question: According to the passage, which factor MOST explains Spanish success in conquering the Aztec Empire?

choices.text: The combination of epidemic disease, indigenous alliances, and European military technology working together
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly rejects monocausal explanations, stating that conquest resulted from multiple factors working in combination — disease, indigenous allies who resented Aztec tribute demands, and European weapons and tactics.

choices.text: Spanish military technology alone, particularly gunpowder weapons and steel armor
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states 'not merely in European military technology' — it argues against a technology-only explanation and emphasizes disease and alliances as equally important.

choices.text: The Aztec belief that Cortés was a returning god, which paralyzed their resistance
choices.explanation: The 'returning god' explanation is a popular myth not supported by most modern historians. The passage does not mention this factor and attributes conquest to disease, alliances, and technology.

choices.text: The numerical superiority of Spanish forces over the Aztec army
choices.explanation: The passage notes Cortés had only ~500 soldiers — the Aztec Empire could field forces of tens of thousands. Spanish success despite numerical inferiority is the puzzle the passage addresses, not numerical superiority.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how and why European powers established overseas colonies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The mita system used in Spanish colonial Peru most closely resembled which earlier Andean institution?

choices.text: The Inca mit'a, a labor tribute system requiring communities to provide labor to the state
choices.explanation: Spanish colonizers adapted the Inca mit'a (rotational labor obligation to the state) into the colonial mita, using it primarily to extract labor from indigenous communities for silver mining at Potosí.

choices.text: The quipu recording system used by Inca administrators to track goods and tribute
choices.explanation: The quipu was a recording device (knotted strings), not a labor system. The mita specifically referred to the labor tribute institution.

choices.text: The encomienda as practiced in the Caribbean and Mexico
choices.explanation: The encomienda was an Iberian institution adapted for the Americas — not an Andean precedent. The mita derived from the pre-colonial Inca labor system.

choices.text: The chattel slavery system imported from West Africa
choices.explanation: The mita was a rotational labor tribute distinct from chattel slavery. Its antecedent was the Inca system of state labor, not Atlantic slavery.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how colonial labor systems developed and changed.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The Portuguese Estado da India was never a territorial empire in the traditional sense. Rather, it was a network of fortified trading posts (feitorias) controlling key choke points on sea lanes — Hormuz, Goa, Malacca, Macao — using naval power to extract tolls from Asian merchants who wished to use 'their' oceans." — Adapted from a modern study of Portuguese expansion

questions.question: How did the Portuguese Estado da India differ from later European colonial empires such as the British Raj in India?

choices.text: The Portuguese prioritized control of sea trade routes and commercial profits rather than territorial administration of large inland areas
choices.explanation: The Estado da India was a maritime commercial empire of coastal fortresses and sea control — not territorial rule over inland populations. The British Raj, by contrast, governed vast interior territories through direct administration and land taxation.

choices.text: The Portuguese relied exclusively on peaceful diplomacy rather than military force to establish trade
choices.explanation: The Portuguese used extensive naval violence — bombarding Calicut, capturing Goa, monopolizing sea routes by force — not peaceful diplomacy. Control of sea lanes was enforced militarily.

choices.text: The Portuguese never established permanent settlements in Asia, operating only from ships
choices.explanation: The passage specifically mentions fortified trading posts (feitorias) at Hormuz, Goa, Malacca, and Macao — permanent coastal settlements that served as the empire's anchors.

choices.text: The Estado da India was primarily a religious mission that used trade as a cover for Catholic conversion
choices.explanation: While religion was part of Portuguese imperial ideology (the padroado system), the Estado da India was primarily a profit-driven commercial operation as the passage describes.

questions.unit_objective: Compare European approaches to colonization in the early modern period.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of mercantilism as practiced by European colonial powers in the 16th–18th centuries?

choices.text: The theory that a nation's wealth was measured by its gold and silver reserves, achieved by maximizing exports and minimizing imports from colonies
choices.explanation: Mercantilism held that wealth was finite and zero-sum, measured in bullion. Colonies existed to supply raw materials and serve as captive markets for manufactured goods, maximizing the mother country's trade surplus and silver accumulation.

choices.text: The belief that free trade between all nations would maximize prosperity for all participants
choices.explanation: This describes Adam Smith's liberal economics, which directly challenged mercantilism. Mercantilism emphasized state control, protective tariffs, and colonial exploitation — the opposite of free trade.

choices.text: The economic system in which colonies were self-governing and set their own trade policies
choices.explanation: Mercantilism gave metropoles strict control over colonial trade through navigation acts and monopolies precisely to prevent colonies from developing independent economic policies.

choices.text: The practice of trading manufactured goods for raw materials on terms negotiated equally by both parties
choices.explanation: Mercantilism was explicitly exploitative — colonies were forced to sell raw materials cheaply and buy manufactured goods at inflated prices, with no equal negotiation.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of European exploration and colonization.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
| Region | Estimated Population 1500 | Estimated Population 1600 | % Change |
|--------|--------------------------|--------------------------|----------|
| Mesoamerica | 25 million | 2.5 million | -90% |
| Caribbean Islands | 1 million | ~0 (near extinction) | -100% |
| Andes | 10 million | 1.5 million | -85% |
| North America | 10 million | 8 million | -20% |

questions.question: Which factor MOST directly explains the population decline shown in the table?

choices.text: Epidemic diseases such as smallpox and measles, to which indigenous populations had no prior immunity
choices.explanation: Indigenous Americans lacked immunity to Old World diseases — smallpox, measles, influenza, typhus — that spread rapidly across interconnected communities. Disease accounted for 50–90% of population decline before sustained European military conquest.

choices.text: Direct military conquest by Spanish forces that killed millions in open battlefield combat
choices.explanation: Military casualties were significant but far smaller than epidemic deaths. Historians estimate disease killed far more people than direct violence. The scale of decline in the table (80–100%) cannot be explained by military conquest alone.

choices.text: Deliberate Spanish policy of exterminating indigenous populations to take their land
choices.explanation: While Spanish colonizers were often brutal, deliberate genocide was not state policy — Spain needed indigenous labor for its colonial economy. Disease, labor exploitation, and food system disruption were the primary killers.

choices.text: Famine caused by the introduction of European crops that replaced indigenous agriculture
choices.explanation: European crops supplemented rather than immediately replaced indigenous agriculture. While disruption contributed, famine from crop replacement alone cannot explain the 85–100% declines shown in the table.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Columbian Exchange.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following was the primary motivation driving European exploration in the 15th and 16th centuries?

choices.text: Access to Asian spices, silk, and luxury goods without paying tolls to Ottoman or Muslim intermediaries
choices.explanation: European demand for Asian goods (pepper, cinnamon, silk, porcelain) was high, but Ottoman control of eastern trade routes added costly middlemen. Finding direct sea routes was the primary economic incentive for exploration.

choices.text: The desire to spread democratic governance to non-European peoples
choices.explanation: Democracy was not a 15th or 16th-century European value — European powers were monarchies. Religious conversion (Christianity) and commercial profit, not democracy, motivated exploration.

choices.text: Overpopulation in Europe that required new settlement territories
choices.explanation: Europe had actually been depopulated by the Black Death. Overpopulation was not a driver of 15th-century exploration — economic and religious motivations were far more important.

choices.text: Scientific curiosity about the shape of the earth and desire to prove it was round
choices.explanation: Educated Europeans already knew the earth was spherical by the 15th century — this is a popular misconception. Commercial profit and trade access were the primary motivations, not geographic curiosity.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of European exploration.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The African societies that participated in the Atlantic slave trade generally did so as sovereign actors pursuing their own interests — some were primarily victims, others were active participants who raided neighbors or sold war captives. The trade cannot be understood solely as something done TO Africa by Europeans." — Adapted from a modern historical analysis

questions.question: The historian's argument in the passage is best used to support which of the following conclusions?

choices.text: African agency in the slave trade complicates a simple victim narrative, but does not diminish European responsibility for creating and sustaining the system
choices.explanation: Historians acknowledge African rulers' participation in slave capture and sale, but argue Europeans created the Atlantic demand, supplied the ships, and built the plantation system that transformed local African practices into a massive intercontinental trade — making European responsibility central even while recognizing African agency.

choices.text: Because African states voluntarily participated, European nations bear no moral responsibility for the slave trade
choices.explanation: Recognizing African agency does not absolve European responsibility — European demand, financing, shipping, and colonial plantation systems created the conditions that incentivized African participation on a massive scale.

choices.text: All African rulers equally opposed the slave trade but were forced to participate through military threats from European traders
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly rejects this — it states some African actors were 'active participants' pursuing their own interests. African responses to the slave trade were diverse, not uniformly coerced resistance.

choices.text: The slave trade had no significant long-term political effects on West African states
choices.explanation: The slave trade profoundly affected West African societies — draining population, fueling internal warfare, and strengthening some states while weakening others. The passage addresses agency, not the absence of impact.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Atlantic slave trade.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: How did the introduction of horses to the Americas after 1492 transform indigenous societies on the Great Plains of North America?

choices.text: It enabled greater mobility for nomadic hunting, dramatically increasing the effectiveness of bison hunting and reshaping Plains societies
choices.explanation: The horse (extinct in the Americas for ~10,000 years before Columbus) spread from Spanish colonies northward, transforming Plains tribes like the Comanche and Sioux into mounted nomadic hunters by the 1700s-1800s — creating new cultures and military power.

choices.text: It allowed Plains peoples to build larger permanent agricultural settlements
choices.explanation: Horses increased mobility for hunting — they did not lead to larger agricultural settlements. Some Plains peoples actually transitioned away from agriculture toward nomadic mounted hunting.

choices.text: It enabled Plains peoples to build ocean-going vessels for trade with European merchants
choices.explanation: Horses transformed land-based transportation and hunting, not seafaring. Plains cultures remained land-based and did not develop ocean commerce.

choices.text: It had no significant impact since Plains peoples had previously domesticated wild horses
choices.explanation: Horses had been extinct in the Americas for approximately 10,000 years before European reintroduction. There were no wild horses for Plains peoples to domesticate prior to 1492.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Columbian Exchange.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "In Cuzco and throughout Peru, when the church bells ring at midnight on Holy Thursday, the people burst into weeping and lamentation... They say it is the hour when the Inca was captured by the Spanish, and they keep alive his memory with tears and sighs, awaiting the day when he will return to rule them again." — Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala, indigenous Peruvian chronicler, c. 1615

questions.question: Guaman Poma's account is most useful as evidence of which historical development?

choices.text: The persistence of indigenous cultural memory and resistance to colonial domination even under Spanish rule
choices.explanation: Guaman Poma documents active indigenous preservation of pre-conquest memory and identity under Spanish colonial rule — the Andean concept of the Inkarri (Inca king who would return) represents cultural continuity and a form of passive resistance.

choices.text: The complete cultural assimilation of Andean peoples into Spanish Catholic society by 1615
choices.explanation: The passage demonstrates the opposite — Andean peoples maintained distinct cultural memory and identity, blending Christian observances with Inca commemoration, reflecting syncretism rather than complete assimilation.

choices.text: The successful military rebellion of indigenous Peruvians against Spanish colonial authority
choices.explanation: The passage describes lamentation and waiting — passive cultural resistance — not active military rebellion. Significant Andean rebellions (like Túpac Amaru II in 1780) came much later.

choices.text: The Spanish Crown's policy of encouraging indigenous peoples to celebrate pre-conquest rulers
choices.explanation: Spanish colonial authorities generally suppressed indigenous religious and cultural practices as idolatry. This gathering represents indigenous cultural persistence despite colonial disapproval, not Spanish encouragement.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how indigenous peoples responded to and were affected by European colonization.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why sugar cultivation in the Caribbean became so closely linked to African slavery?

choices.text: Indigenous Caribbean populations were decimated by disease, creating a severe labor shortage that planters filled with enslaved Africans, who were seen as more resistant to tropical diseases
choices.explanation: The near-total collapse of Caribbean indigenous populations within decades of contact (up to 100% in some islands) created an acute labor shortage. Planters turned to African enslaved labor, also believing (partly incorrectly) that Africans were more resistant to tropical diseases.

choices.text: African laborers were specifically trained in sugar cultivation in Africa before being brought to the Americas
choices.explanation: Most enslaved Africans had no prior experience with sugar — sugar cultivation was learned in the Americas. They were enslaved because of labor scarcity and racism, not because of pre-existing sugar expertise.

choices.text: Sugar could only be grown in climates identical to West Africa, making African laborers uniquely suited to Caribbean conditions
choices.explanation: While there is a racial mythology of African 'fitness' for tropical labor (used to justify slavery), sugar cultivation spread wherever warm, wet climates existed. The connection to African labor was driven by labor scarcity and colonial economics, not climate-specific fitness.

choices.text: African rulers demanded that Europeans purchase their enslaved captives to plant in Caribbean sugar fields
choices.explanation: While some African rulers sold war captives to European traders, the plantation system and its demand for enslaved labor was driven by European colonial economics — not African rulers' demands for Caribbean sugar cultivation specifically.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Atlantic slave trade.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The Treaty of Tordesillas (1494) divided the non-Christian world between Spain and Portugal along a meridian 370 leagues west of the Cape Verde Islands. Portugal received Africa, Asia, and Brazil; Spain received the rest of the Americas." — Adapted from a historical description

questions.question: The Treaty of Tordesillas is most significant as evidence of which historical development?

choices.text: The role of the Catholic Church in legitimizing European claims to colonize non-Christian territories
choices.explanation: The Treaty was brokered by Pope Alexander VI, reflecting the idea that the papacy had authority to divide the non-Christian world between Catholic powers. This papal donation became a foundational legal fiction justifying European colonialism.

choices.text: The establishment of genuinely equal partnership between Spain and Portugal in colonial enterprises
choices.explanation: The treaty divided the world asymmetrically based on existing Portuguese advantages in Africa/Asia — it was a negotiated settlement reflecting competing imperial interests, not equal partnership in shared enterprises.

choices.text: The universal recognition by all European powers of Spanish and Portuguese colonial authority
choices.explanation: France, England, and the Netherlands famously rejected the Treaty of Tordesillas — France's King Francis I reportedly asked to see Adam's will giving all the world to Spain and Portugal. The treaty was only binding on its two signatories.

choices.text: The first international agreement in history to define national boundaries using geographic meridians
choices.explanation: While historically significant, the significance AP World History emphasizes is its role in legitimizing colonial expansion through papal authority — not its cartographic innovation, which was not unprecedented.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how and why European powers established overseas colonies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of 'syncretism' as it developed in the colonial Americas?

choices.text: The blending of indigenous, African, and European religious and cultural elements into new hybrid practices
choices.explanation: Syncretism in colonial Americas produced hybrid cultural forms — like Santería (African-Catholic blend), Day of the Dead (indigenous-Catholic blend), or the Virgin of Guadalupe (indigenous-Catholic religious synthesis) — as peoples combined their traditions under colonial conditions.

choices.text: The complete replacement of indigenous cultures by European Christianity and Spanish customs
choices.explanation: Syncretism specifically describes the blending of traditions, not their complete replacement. Indigenous and African elements persisted and merged with European Christianity rather than being fully erased.

choices.text: The official Spanish policy of encouraging multiple religions to coexist equally in colonial territories
choices.explanation: Spain enforced Catholicism and used the Inquisition to police religious conformity — there was no official policy of religious pluralism. Syncretism emerged from indigenous and African peoples' responses to forced conversion, not Spanish tolerance.

choices.text: The transfer of European philosophical ideas to the Americas that rejected religious belief entirely
choices.explanation: Colonial-era syncretism was emphatically religious in character — it involved blending religious traditions, not replacing religion with secular philosophy. That development came later with the Enlightenment.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how the exchange of ideas, commodities, diseases, and people transformed societies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "With the opening of the silver mines at Potosí and Zacatecas in the mid-16th century, American silver flooded Eurasian markets. By 1600, roughly 50% of all silver in world circulation came from Spanish American mines. This silver lubricated global commerce — from Manila to Lisbon to Beijing — transforming economies on every continent." — Adapted from a global history textbook

questions.question: According to the passage, what was the most significant global consequence of American silver production?

choices.text: Silver became a global currency that connected and integrated markets across multiple continents
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states silver 'lubricated global commerce from Manila to Lisbon to Beijing' — integrating economies across Eurasia. American silver was the monetary glue that made genuinely global trade possible for the first time.

choices.text: Spain used silver to fund the complete military conquest of Asia and Africa
choices.explanation: Spain did not conquer Asia or Africa. Silver funded Spanish European military campaigns and imported Asian luxury goods — it was a commercial medium, not a tool for Asian/African conquest.

choices.text: Silver's abundance caused European economies to collapse due to massive deflation
choices.explanation: Silver abundance caused inflation (the 'Price Revolution'), not deflation. More silver chasing the same goods raised prices across Europe and parts of Asia.

choices.text: The Ming Dynasty used American silver to fund the construction of the Great Wall
choices.explanation: The Great Wall's construction predates major American silver imports. Silver imports did fund Ming government operations and fuel commercial growth, but were not specifically directed at Great Wall construction.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how the silver trade connected global economies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: How did the British East India Company's role in India differ from the early Portuguese Estado da India in terms of colonial administration?

choices.text: The British East India Company gradually assumed territorial governance over large inland areas, collecting taxes and administering justice, while the Portuguese focused on coastal trade control
choices.explanation: The EIC evolved from a trading company to a territorial power after Plassey (1757), governing Bengal and eventually much of India directly. The Portuguese Estado da India maintained coastal fortresses for trade control without large-scale inland administration.

choices.text: The British East India Company operated exclusively as a charitable organization focused on spreading Christianity
choices.explanation: The EIC was a profit-driven joint-stock company — one of history's most commercially aggressive corporations. Religion was secondary to commerce and eventually territorial power.

choices.text: The Portuguese Estado da India governed large territories while the British East India Company only traded in coastal ports
choices.explanation: This reverses the historical reality. The Portuguese maintained coastal outposts; the British eventually governed vast inland territories.

choices.text: Both companies administered identical colonial systems based on the encomienda model from Spanish America
choices.explanation: The encomienda was a Spanish American institution. Portuguese and British colonial structures in Asia were distinct — neither adopted the encomienda, which was specific to Spanish American indigenous labor extraction.

questions.unit_objective: Compare European approaches to colonization in the early modern period.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the Columbian Exchange had a greater demographic impact on the Americas than on Europe?

choices.text: Europeans carried diseases to which they had partial immunity through prior exposure, while indigenous Americans had no immunity to Old World pathogens
choices.explanation: Centuries of living with domesticated animals gave Eurasians partial immunity to diseases like smallpox and measles. Native Americans, isolated for millennia with fewer domesticated animals, had zero prior exposure and thus no immunity, leading to catastrophic death rates of 80–90%.

choices.text: Indigenous Americans had inferior diets that made them more vulnerable to disease than Europeans
choices.explanation: Indigenous Americans often had nutritionally rich diets (maize, beans, squash, etc.). Vulnerability to Old World diseases was not due to dietary inferiority but to lack of prior immunological exposure.

choices.text: European diseases were more lethal than American diseases in all biological contexts
choices.explanation: European diseases were more lethal to Americans because of immunological naivety, not because they were inherently more lethal. The same diseases caused severe but not catastrophic mortality in populations with prior exposure.

choices.text: The Americas had fewer people before contact, making disease spread faster in less populated areas
choices.explanation: The Americas had tens of millions of people before contact. Dense populations (like central Mexico) actually facilitated rapid disease spread. Population density is not the explanation — immunological naivety is.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Columbian Exchange.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The abolitionists argued that slavery was morally wrong and contrary to Christian principles. The planters responded that slavery was economically necessary, legally established, and that enslaved people were better off under Christian masters than in 'barbaric' Africa." — Adapted from an 18th-century debate

questions.question: The planters' argument in the passage is best understood as an example of which broader historical phenomenon?

choices.text: The use of religious and racial ideology to justify economically motivated exploitation of enslaved peoples
choices.explanation: The planters combined paternalistic racism ('better off under Christian masters') with religious justification to defend a system that existed because enslaved labor was economically profitable — illustrating how ideology was constructed to legitimize exploitation.

choices.text: A genuine theological debate within Christianity about the ethics of slavery
choices.explanation: While some theological debate occurred, the planter argument was primarily a defense of economic interests dressed in religious language — not a sincere theological inquiry. The economic stakes shaped the theological conclusions.

choices.text: The gradual acceptance by enslaved people of their condition as legitimate
choices.explanation: The passage presents the planters' self-serving justification — it is not evidence that enslaved people accepted their condition. Resistance to slavery was constant and took many forms.

choices.text: A uniquely European form of racism that had no counterpart in other slave-holding societies
choices.explanation: Slavery existed in many societies worldwide, often with ideological justifications. European Atlantic slavery developed a particularly elaborate racial ideology tied to skin color, but ideological justifications for slavery were not uniquely European.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Atlantic slave trade.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The establishment of the plantation complex in the Caribbean most directly required which combination of factors?

choices.text: European capital investment, enslaved African labor, and American land cleared of indigenous populations
choices.explanation: The plantation system was the intersection of European capital (funding cultivation and trade), African enslaved labor (replacing decimated indigenous populations), and American land (cleared by disease and conquest) — three components of the Atlantic World triangle.

choices.text: Ottoman merchants, Chinese agricultural technology, and indigenous American knowledge of sugar cultivation
choices.explanation: The plantation complex was an Atlantic system — Ottoman merchants and Chinese technology were not components. Sugar cultivation knowledge came from earlier Portuguese/Spanish Atlantic islands (Madeira, São Tomé), not indigenous Americans.

choices.text: Free wage labor from Europe, local Caribbean food crops, and Protestant missionary activity
choices.explanation: Plantations used enslaved labor (not free wages), monoculture cash crops for export (not local food crops), and Catholic or Protestant religion was secondary to commercial motives.

choices.text: African gold, indigenous technology, and European population surplus seeking new farmland
choices.explanation: European settlers did not primarily seek farmland — they sought plantation profits. The plantation system required enslaved labor, not a settler farming model, and African gold was not the key resource.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Atlantic slave trade.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Francisco Pizarro's conquest of the Inca Empire in the 1530s was most aided by which of the following factors?

choices.text: A devastating smallpox epidemic that had already killed the Inca emperor and triggered a civil war before Spanish arrival
choices.explanation: Smallpox, transmitted ahead of Spanish forces along trade networks, killed the Inca emperor Huayna Capac around 1527, triggering a devastating civil war between his sons Huáscar and Atahualpa that Pizarro exploited upon arrival.

choices.text: A large Spanish army of over 10,000 soldiers supported by the Portuguese Navy
choices.explanation: Pizarro had only about 168 soldiers. Portugal was a rival, not an ally in Andean conquest. Spanish success with tiny forces is explained by disease, civil war, and indigenous alliances — not large armies.

choices.text: The Inca Empire's decision to voluntarily surrender in exchange for Christian conversion
choices.explanation: The Inca did not voluntarily surrender. Pizarro captured Atahualpa through treachery at Cajamarca, executed him despite receiving a ransom, and conquered through force — not voluntary conversion.

choices.text: The widespread adoption of European military tactics by Spanish-trained Andean soldiers
choices.explanation: The Spanish used indigenous allies (with traditional weapons and tactics) alongside their own soldiers. The key factors were disease, political disruption, and surprise — not European-trained Andean soldiers.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how European powers established overseas colonies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "Sugar plantations in Brazil and the Caribbean operated on a logic of extreme intensity: enslaved workers were worked to death in 7–10 years and replaced with new imports from Africa. This 'mortality economics' made continuous slave imports economically rational to planters, even as it consumed millions of lives." — Adapted from a modern economic history

questions.question: The 'mortality economics' described in the passage best explains which aspect of the Atlantic slave trade?

choices.text: Why the slave trade continued at high volume for centuries despite the enormous human cost — because planters calculated replacement cheaper than humane treatment
choices.explanation: The brutality of sugar plantations was not accidental — it was economically calculated. Working enslaved people to death in 7–10 years and importing replacements was cheaper than providing conditions for longer survival and natural population growth, sustaining massive slave imports for over 300 years.

choices.text: Why planters preferred European indentured servants over African enslaved workers on sugar plantations
choices.explanation: The passage describes the opposite preference — African enslaved labor was used precisely because it could be worked at intensities that killed workers in years, then replaced at low cost through continued imports.

choices.text: Why the Catholic Church opposed the Atlantic slave trade from its beginning
choices.explanation: The Catholic Church did not uniformly oppose the slave trade from its beginning — many church figures defended or participated in it. Some individuals criticized it (like Las Casas), but institutional opposition developed much later.

choices.text: Why enslaved people in sugar-producing regions had higher survival rates than those in tobacco colonies
choices.explanation: The passage describes mortality economics showing LOWER survival rates in sugar colonies — enslaved people on sugar plantations had shorter life expectancies than those in tobacco, rice, or cotton regions, not higher.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Atlantic slave trade.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of the Catholic Church in Spanish colonial society in the Americas?

choices.text: The Church served simultaneously as an agent of cultural repression and as a source of indigenous protection and legal advocacy
choices.explanation: The Church oversaw forced conversion, destroyed indigenous temples and texts (Landa's burning of Maya books), and operated the Inquisition — but also produced advocates like Las Casas who defended indigenous rights and established the New Laws of 1542.

choices.text: The Church maintained strict neutrality in colonial affairs to avoid conflicts with the Spanish Crown
choices.explanation: The Church was deeply embedded in colonial governance through the Padroado system — it was far from neutral. Bishops held political power and church institutions like the encomienda system were intertwined with colonial authority.

choices.text: The Church exclusively protected indigenous peoples from Spanish colonist exploitation
choices.explanation: The Church's role was deeply contradictory — it participated in exploitation through its own landholdings and labor demands while also producing advocates for indigenous rights. Presenting it as exclusively protective is inaccurate.

choices.text: The Church operated completely independently of the Spanish Crown with no political influence
choices.explanation: Through the Patronato system, the Spanish Crown actually controlled church appointments in the colonies. The Church and Crown were deeply interdependent in colonial administration.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how indigenous peoples responded to and were affected by European colonization.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "I, being informed that certain priests took from the Indians their offerings and alms, saying they were for the souls of the dead... I order that no priest shall take from any Indian anything in the name of offerings for dead souls, nor for baptisms, nor for marriages, nor for any other sacrament." — Viceroy of New Spain, Ordinance, 1574

questions.question: The ordinance above is best understood as evidence of which tension in Spanish colonial society?

choices.text: Conflicts between the Spanish Crown's attempts to regulate clergy behavior and the exploitation of indigenous peoples by colonial priests
choices.explanation: The viceroy's ordinance reveals that priests were systematically extracting tribute from indigenous peoples under religious pretexts — and that the Crown periodically attempted to regulate such exploitation, reflecting ongoing tension between Crown authority and colonial church actors.

choices.text: Indigenous peoples' rejection of Catholic sacraments in favor of traditional religious practices
choices.explanation: The ordinance addresses clerical exploitation of indigenous people who were participating in Catholic practices (accepting sacraments, making offerings) — not indigenous rejection of Catholicism.

choices.text: Competition between the Spanish Crown and the Pope for control of religious taxes in the colonies
choices.explanation: The conflict described is between the viceroy (representing Crown authority) and local colonial clergy, not between the Crown and the papacy over taxation policy.

choices.text: The success of indigenous peoples in using Spanish legal systems to protect their communities from exploitation
choices.explanation: The ordinance was issued by a viceroy acting on the Crown's behalf — it represents top-down regulation, not indigenous legal activism, though indigenous peoples did sometimes use colonial courts.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how social hierarchies developed in European colonial societies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why tobacco cultivation in the Chesapeake region of North America initially relied on European indentured servants rather than African enslaved labor?

choices.text: African enslaved labor was more expensive and less available in the early 17th century, while indentured servants from England were abundant and cheaper
choices.explanation: In the early 1600s, English indentured servants were cheaper to acquire than enslaved Africans, who required larger upfront purchase costs. As servants' terms expired and the supply dwindled, and as African slave prices dropped with expanded trade, planters shifted to enslaved African labor by the late 1600s.

choices.text: English colonial law prohibited the use of African enslaved labor in North America until 1700
choices.explanation: There was no blanket prohibition. Enslaved Africans were present in Virginia from 1619, though their legal status was initially ambiguous. Slavery was gradually codified into law through the late 1600s.

choices.text: Indigenous peoples successfully performed all agricultural labor in the Chesapeake region until the 1700s
choices.explanation: Disease and resistance meant indigenous peoples were not a reliable labor source for English colonists. The labor system relied on European servants and then African enslaved workers, not indigenous agricultural labor.

choices.text: Tobacco required skilled knowledge that only European craftsmen possessed, making African labor unsuitable
choices.explanation: Tobacco cultivation required no specialized European knowledge — it was a crop indigenous to the Americas. Enslaved Africans eventually dominated tobacco cultivation once the economic calculus shifted.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how colonial labor systems developed and changed.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The Columbian Exchange included which of the following transfers FROM the Americas TO Europe?

choices.text: Potatoes, tomatoes, and cacao (chocolate)
choices.explanation: Potatoes, tomatoes, cacao, maize, tobacco, vanilla, and chili peppers all originated in the Americas and were transferred to Europe (and beyond) after Columbus's voyages, transforming European diets and agriculture.

choices.text: Wheat, grapes, and cattle
choices.explanation: Wheat, grapes, and cattle are Old World domesticates that traveled FROM Europe to the Americas — not from the Americas to Europe.

choices.text: Rice, sugarcane, and horses
choices.explanation: Rice originated in Asia, sugarcane in South/Southeast Asia or New Guinea, and horses in Eurasia (extinct in Americas) — all traveled from the Old World to the Americas, not vice versa.

choices.text: Coffee, silk, and spices
choices.explanation: Coffee originated in Ethiopia (Africa), silk in China, and spices in South/Southeast Asia — none originate in the Americas. These were established Old World trade commodities.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Columbian Exchange.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "In the 16th and 17th centuries, Japan, China, and some Muslim polities imposed restrictions on European trade — forcing Europeans to operate only through designated ports (Canton, Nagasaki) or banning them entirely. Yet trade continued because both sides found it economically beneficial." — Adapted from a modern global history

questions.question: The pattern described in the passage best supports which conclusion about Asian responses to European commercial expansion?

choices.text: Asian states were not passive recipients of European power — they actively shaped the terms and conditions under which Europeans could trade within their territories
choices.explanation: Ming/Qing China's Canton system, Japan's Dutch-only Deshima trading post, and Mughal regulation of European merchants all demonstrate Asian states exercising sovereignty over European commercial access — challenging the narrative of European dominance.

choices.text: European powers chose to limit their own trade with Asia to prevent the spread of Christianity
choices.explanation: European powers sought maximum trade access in Asia — they did not voluntarily limit themselves. Restrictions like Canton or Nagasaki were imposed by Asian states, not chosen by Europeans.

choices.text: Asian states had no significant military or commercial power to resist European expansion in the 16th and 17th centuries
choices.explanation: Ming China, the Mughal Empire, the Ottoman Empire, and Tokugawa Japan all possessed sufficient military and commercial power to regulate or restrict European access. European dominance in Asia came later.

choices.text: Trade restrictions imposed by Asian states caused European merchants to abandon Asian markets entirely
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states trade continued despite restrictions — both sides found it beneficial. European merchants adapted to restricted access rather than abandoning profitable Asian trade.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how non-European states responded to European expansion.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best describes how the triangular trade system worked in the Atlantic World?

choices.text: Europe sent manufactured goods to Africa for enslaved people; enslaved Africans were transported to the Americas as labor; American raw materials (sugar, tobacco, cotton) returned to Europe
choices.explanation: The triangular trade connected three continents: European manufactures (textiles, weapons, alcohol) to Africa; enslaved Africans across the Middle Passage to the Americas; colonial raw materials back to European markets — creating a self-reinforcing commercial system.

choices.text: Asia sent spices to Africa; Africa sent gold to Europe; Europe sent silver to Asia
choices.explanation: This describes aspects of Indian Ocean and Mediterranean trade, not the triangular Atlantic trade. The triangular trade specifically connected Europe, Africa, and the Americas through enslaved labor and plantation products.

choices.text: The Americas sent silver to Europe; Europe sent missionaries to Asia; Asia sent silk to Africa
choices.explanation: This describes unconnected trade flows across different ocean basins. The triangular trade was specifically the Atlantic circuit connecting Europe, Africa, and the Americas through the slave trade and plantation goods.

choices.text: European nations traded finished goods with each other while sourcing all raw materials from within Europe
choices.explanation: The entire point of the Atlantic colonial system was that European powers extracted raw materials from colonies — not from within Europe. Intra-European trade was separate from the colonial triangular trade.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Atlantic slave trade.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The introduction of the potato to Ireland transformed Irish agriculture. By 1800, the potato had become the primary food for the rural poor, providing calories far exceeding any other crop per acre. This dependence made Irish peasants highly vulnerable to crop failure, as the Famine of 1845–1852 would demonstrate." — Adapted from a modern agricultural history

questions.question: The passage is most useful as evidence for which historical argument about the Columbian Exchange?

choices.text: The Columbian Exchange could create both population growth and dangerous agricultural dependency simultaneously
choices.explanation: The potato (an American crop) fed Irish population growth for over a century while creating monoculture dependency that made catastrophic famine possible when blight struck. This illustrates the Columbian Exchange's complex, double-edged consequences.

choices.text: The Columbian Exchange had exclusively positive effects on European agricultural productivity
choices.explanation: The passage itself demonstrates a catastrophic downside — Irish dependence on the potato enabled the Great Famine that killed ~1 million and drove ~1 million to emigrate. The Columbian Exchange's effects were mixed.

choices.text: American crops were never successfully adopted outside of the Americas due to soil incompatibility
choices.explanation: This directly contradicts the passage, which documents the potato's transformative adoption in Ireland. American crops were widely adopted across Europe, Africa, and Asia.

choices.text: The Columbian Exchange's impact was limited to the 16th century and had no long-term consequences for European society
choices.explanation: The passage documents consequences extending to the 19th century (Irish Famine, 1845–1852). The Columbian Exchange had profound long-term demographic, agricultural, and cultural consequences for centuries.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Columbian Exchange.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: How did the role of the mestizo population in Spanish colonial society change over the course of the colonial period?

choices.text: Mestizos grew in number and gradually occupied intermediate social and economic positions, sometimes achieving mobility unavailable to indigenous or African peoples
choices.explanation: As the mixed-race population expanded, mestizos occupied artisan, commercial, and lower administrative roles that positioned them between European elites and indigenous/African peoples — creating a complex intermediary class in colonial society.

choices.text: Mestizos were legally classified as indigenous and subject to all indigenous tribute obligations
choices.explanation: Mestizos occupied a distinct legal category from indigenous peoples — they were generally exempt from indigenous tribute obligations, though they faced discrimination compared to criollos and peninsulares.

choices.text: Mestizos were expelled from Spanish colonial territories due to their mixed ancestry
choices.explanation: There was no policy of expelling mestizos — they became a large and growing segment of colonial populations. Colonial policy attempted to categorize and control them, not expel them.

choices.text: Mestizos held the highest social positions in colonial society because they could serve as cultural intermediaries between Spanish and indigenous worlds
choices.explanation: Mestizos held intermediate social positions, not the highest. Peninsulares and criollos of 'pure' European ancestry held the highest-status positions in colonial social hierarchies.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how social hierarchies developed in European colonial societies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "One of the most striking facts about early modern globalization is that its primary driver was not cotton, grain, or manufactured goods, but a single metal: silver. Without silver to pay for Asian luxuries that Europeans craved but Asians rarely wanted from Europeans in return, the early global economy would have ground to a halt." — Adapted from a world economic history

questions.question: The historian's argument challenges which common assumption about early modern European commercial expansion?

choices.text: That European manufactured goods were competitive with Asian products and drove global trade through commercial superiority
choices.explanation: The passage reveals that Europeans could not sell their manufactured goods in Asian markets — Asians didn't want European products. Only silver (extracted through colonial violence in the Americas) enabled Europeans to purchase the Asian goods they craved, revealing a fundamental European commercial weakness underlying apparent expansion.

choices.text: That silver mining in the Americas was an important economic activity
choices.explanation: The importance of American silver mining is well-established and not a 'common assumption' that needs challenging. The passage's insight is about WHY silver was so critical — to solve the European trade imbalance with Asia.

choices.text: That global trade in the early modern period was entirely controlled by Asian merchants
choices.explanation: While the passage acknowledges Asian commercial strength, the argument is specifically about the role of silver in enabling European-Asian trade — not a broader claim about who 'entirely controlled' global trade.

choices.text: That the Americas had no valuable resources before European colonization
choices.explanation: The value of American silver is not a 'common assumption' being challenged — it's well known. The passage's argument is more specific: that silver was the essential lubricant of early global trade, not merely one resource among many.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how the silver trade connected global economies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the sugar plantation system eventually shifted from using indigenous Caribbean labor to African enslaved labor?

choices.text: Epidemic diseases killed the vast majority of indigenous Caribbean peoples within decades of contact, creating an acute labor shortage that planters filled with enslaved Africans
choices.explanation: Caribbean indigenous populations (Taíno, Carib) collapsed by 50–100% within 50 years of contact due to smallpox, measles, influenza, and overwork. Planters then turned to West Africa's established slave trade networks to supply replacement labor.

choices.text: Indigenous Caribbean peoples voluntarily left the islands to trade with North American tribes, abandoning plantation work
choices.explanation: Indigenous Caribbean population loss was caused by disease, violence, and overwork — not voluntary migration. Their communities collapsed catastrophically, they did not simply leave.

choices.text: African peoples had traditionally cultivated sugar in West Africa and were therefore uniquely qualified for Caribbean plantations
choices.explanation: Sugar cultivation was not a traditional West African practice. Enslaved Africans were brought for their capacity for labor in general, not for specific sugar expertise.

choices.text: The Spanish Crown specifically prohibited the use of indigenous labor in the Caribbean after 1500
choices.explanation: There was no blanket prohibition — Spanish law regulated but permitted indigenous labor through the encomienda. The shift to African labor was driven primarily by indigenous population collapse, not legal prohibition.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Atlantic slave trade.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following accurately describes the encomienda system in Spanish colonial America?

choices.text: A grant of indigenous labor and tribute to a Spanish colonist, who was theoretically obligated to provide Christian instruction in return
choices.explanation: The encomienda granted colonists (encomenderos) the right to extract labor and tribute from an assigned indigenous community, with the legal fiction that they would provide protection and Christian conversion in return — a reciprocal obligation rarely honored.

choices.text: A land grant system that gave indigenous peoples legal title to Spanish-controlled territories
choices.explanation: The encomienda granted colonists rights over labor and tribute from indigenous peoples — not land title to indigenous peoples. Indigenous land rights were frequently violated, not protected, by the encomienda system.

choices.text: A tax paid by Spanish colonists to the Crown for the right to trade in the Americas
choices.explanation: The encomienda was a labor/tribute grant to colonists, not a tax they paid to the Crown. The Crown did extract various taxes (quinto real = 20% of mining proceeds), but the encomienda was a different institution.
